elements.sent-2411-001: Training reinforcement learning (RL) agents on robotic tasks typically requires a large number of training samples.
elements.sent-2411-002: This is because training data often consists of noisy trajectories, whether from exploration or human-collected demonstrations, making it difficult to learn value functions that understand the effect of taking each action.
elements.sent-2411-003: On the other hand, recent behavior-cloning (BC) approaches have shown that predicting a sequence of actions enables policies to effectively approximate noisy, multi-modal distributions of expert demonstrations.
elements.sent-2411-004: Can we use a similar idea for improving RL on robotic tasks? In this paper, we introduce a novel RL algorithm that learns a critic network that outputs Q-values over a sequence of actions.
elements.sent-2411-005: By explicitly training the value functions to learn the consequence of executing a series of current and future actions, our algorithm allows for learning useful value functions from noisy trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-006: We study our algorithm across various setups with sparse and dense rewards, and with or without demonstrations, spanning mobile bi-manual manipulation, whole-body control, and tabletop manipulation tasks from BiGym, HumanoidBench, and RLBench.
elements.sent-2411-007: We find that, by learning the critic network with action sequences, our algorithm outperforms various RL and BC baselines, in particular on challenging humanoid control tasks.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.99\linewidth]{figures/results/aggregate.pdf} \caption{Summary of results. Coarse-to-fine Q-Network with Action Sequence (CQN-AS) is a value-based RL algorithm that learns a critic network with action sequence. We study CQN-AS on 53 robotic tasks from BiGym \citep{chernyadev2024bigym}, HumanoidBench \citep{sferrazza2024humanoidbench}, and RLBench \citep{james2020rlbench}, where prior model-free RL algorithms struggle to achieve competitive performance. We show that CQN-AS outperforms various RL and BC baselines such as CQN \citep{seo2024continuous}, DrQ-v2+ \citep{yarats2022mastering}, SAC \citep{haarnoja2018soft}, and ACT \citep{zhao2023learning}.} \label{fig:c2f_teaser} \label{fig:summary_of_results}
elements.sent-2411-009: Reinforcement learning (RL) holds the promise of continually improving policies through online trial-and-error experiences \citep{sutton2018reinforcement , making it an ideal choice for developing robots that can adapt to various environments.}
elements.sent-2411-010: However, despite this promise, training RL agents on robotic tasks typically requires a prohibitively large number of training samples \citep{kalashnikov2018scalable,herzog2023deep , which becomes problematic as deploying robots often incurs a huge cost.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Therefore many of the recent successful approaches on robot learning have been based on behavior-cloning (BC; \citealt{pomerleau1988alvinn ), which can learn strong policies from offline expert demonstrations \citep{brohan2022rt1,brohan2023rt,zhao2023learning,chi2023diffusion,team2024octo,fu2024humanplus .}}
elements.sent-2411-012: One cause for the poor data-efficiency of RL algorithms on robotic tasks is that training data consists of noisy trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-013: When collecting data for training RL agents, we typically inject some noise into actions for exploration \citep{sehnke2010parameter,lillicrap2016continuous that may induce trajectories with jerky motions.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Moreover, we often initialize training with human-collected demonstrations that can consist of noisy multi-modal trajectories \citep{chernyadev2024bigym .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Such noisy data distributions make it difficult to learn value functions that should understand the consequence of taking each action.
elements.sent-2411-016: We indeed find that prior RL algorithms perform much worse than the BC baseline on mobile bi-manual manipulation tasks with human-collected demonstrations when compared to a tabletop manipulation setup with synthetic demonstrations collected via motion-planning (see \cref{fig:summary_of_results ).}
elements.sent-2411-017: On the other hand, recent BC approaches have shown that predicting a sequence of actions enables policies to effectively approximate the noisy, multi-modal distribution of expert demonstrations \citep{zhao2023learning,chi2023diffusion .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Inspired by this, in this paper, we investigate how to use a similar idea for improving the data-efficiency of RL algorithms on robotic tasks.
elements.sent-2411-019: In particular, we present a novel RL algorithm that learns a critic network that outputs Q-values over a sequence of actions (see \cref{fig:cqn_as_overview ).}
elements.sent-2411-020: By training the critic network to explicitly learn the consequence of taking a series of current and future actions, our algorithm enables the RL agents to effectively learn useful value functions from noisy trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-021: We build this algorithm upon a recent value-based RL algorithm that learns RL agents to zoom-into continuous action space in a coarse-to-fine manner \citep{seo2024continuous , thus we refer to our algorithm as Coarse-to-fine Q-Network with Action Sequence (CQN-AS).}
elements.sent-2411-022: To evaluate the generality and capabilities of CQN-AS, we study CQN-AS on various setups with sparse and dense rewards, and with or without demonstrations.
elements.sent-2411-023: In BiGym benchmark \citep{chernyadev2024bigym , which provides human-collected demonstrations for mobile bi-manual manipulation tasks, CQN-AS outperforms various model-free RL and BC baselines \citep{yarats2022mastering,zhao2023learning,seo2024continuous .}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Moreover, in HumanoidBench \citep{sferrazza2024humanoidbench , which consists of densely-rewarded humanoid control tasks, we show that CQN-AS can also be effective without demonstrations, outperforming prior model-free RL baselines \citep{haarnoja2018soft,seo2024continuous .}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Finally, in RLBench \citep{james2020rlbench , which provides synthetic demonstrations generated via motion-planning, CQN-AS achieves similar performance as model-free RL and BC baselines on most tasks, but significantly better performance on several long-horizon manipulation tasks.}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.99\textwidth]{figures/high_level/cqn_as_method.pdf} \caption{ Coarse-to-fine Q-network with action sequence. (Left) Our key idea is to train a critic network to output Q-values over a sequence of actions. We design our architecture to first obtain features for each sequence step and aggregate features from multiple sequence steps with a recurrent network. We then project these outputs into Q-values at level $l$. (Right) For action inference, we repeat the procedure of computing Q-values for level $l \in \{1, ..., L\}$. We then find the action sequence with the highest Q-values from the last level $L$, and use it for controlling robots at each time step. } \label{fig:cqn_as_overview}

elements.sent-2411-037: Coarse-to-fine Q-Network (CQN; \citealt{seo2024continuous ) is a value-based RL algorithm for continuous control that trains RL agents to zoom-into the continuous action space in a coarse-to-fine manner.}
elements.sent-2411-038: In particular, CQN iterates the procedures of (i) discretizing the continuous action space into multiple bins and (ii) selecting the bin with the highest Q-value to further discretize.
elements.sent-2411-039: This reformulates the continuous control problem as a multi-level discrete control problem, allowing for the use of ideas from sample-efficient value-based RL algorithms \citep{mnih2015human,silver2017mastering,schrittwieser2020mastering , designed to be used with discrete actions, for continuous control.}

elements.sent-2411-042: We then define the coarse-to-fine critic to consist of multiple Q-networks which compute Q-values for actions at each level $\mathbf{a _{t ^{l}$, given the features $\mathbf{h _{t}$ and actions from the previous level $\mathbf{a _{t ^{l-1}$, as follows: \begin{equation}Q_{\theta}^{l}(h_{t}, a_{t}^{l}, a_{t}^{l-1}) \;\; for \;\; l \in \{1, ..., L\} \label{eq:cqn_formulation}\end{equation} We optimize each Q-network at level $l$ with the following objective: \begin{equation}L^{l} = \left( Q_{\theta}^{l}(h_{t}, a_{t}^{l}, a_{t}^{l-1}) - r_{t+1} - \gamma \max_{a'}Q_{\bar{\theta}}^{l}(h_{t+1}, a', \pi^{l}(h_{t+1}) \right), \label{eq:cqn_objective}\end{equation} where $\bar{\theta}$ are delayed parameters for a target network \citep{polyak1992acceleration and $\pi^{l}$ is a policy that outputs the action $\mathbf{a _{t ^{l}$ at each level $l$ via the inference steps with our critic, i.e., $\pi^{l (\mathbf{h _{t ) = \mathbf{a _{t ^{l}$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-046: Step 2 (Bin selection): Find a bin with the highest Q-value and set $a_{t ^{l}$ to the centroid of the bin.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Step 3 (Zoom-in): Set $a_{t ^{\tt{low }$ and $a_{t ^{\tt{high }$ to the minimum and maximum of the selected bin, which intuitively can be seen as zooming-into each bin.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-050: We present Coarse-to-fine Q-Network with Action Sequence (CQN-AS), a value-based RL algorithm that learns a critic network that outputs Q-values for a sequence of actions $\mathbf{a _{t:t+K = \{\mathbf{a _{t , ..., \mathbf{a _{t+K-1}\}$ for a given observation $\mathbf{o _{t}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: Our main motivation for this design comes from one of the key ideas in recent behavior-cloning (BC) approaches, i.e., predicting action sequences, which helps resolve ambiguity when approximating noisy, multi-modal distributions of expert demonstrations \citep{zhao2023learning,chi2023diffusion .}
elements.sent-2411-052: Similarly, by explicitly learning Q-values of both current and future actions from the given state, our approach aims to mitigate the challenge of learning Q-values with noisy trajectories from exploratory behaviors or human-collected demonstrations.
elements.sent-2411-053: This section describes how we design our critic network with action sequence (see \cref{sec:c2f_critic_as ) and how we utilize action sequence outputs to control robots at each time step (see \cref{sec:controlling_robots ).}}
elements.sent-2411-054: The overview of our algorithm is available in \cref{fig:cqn_as_overview .}
elements.sent-2411-055: Our key idea is to design a critic network to explicitly learn Q-values for current action and future actions from the current time step $t$, i.e., $\{Q(\mathbf{o _{t , \mathbf{a _{t ), Q(\mathbf{o _{t , \mathbf{a _{t+1 ), ..., Q(\mathbf{o _{t , \mathbf{a _{t+K-1 )\}$, to enable the critic to understand the consequence of executing a series of actions from the given state.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: Let $\mathbf{a _{t:t+K ^{l = \{ \mathbf{a _{t ^{l , ..., \mathbf{a _{t+K-1 ^{l}\}$ be an action sequence at level $l$ and $\mathbf{a _{t:t+K ^{0}$ be a zero vector.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: We design our coarse-to-fine critic network to consist of multiple Q-networks that compute Q-values for each action at sequence step $k$ and level $l$: \begin{equation}Q_{\theta}^{l, k}(h_{t}, a_{t+k-1}^{l}, a_{t:t+K}^{l-1}) \; for \;\; l \in \{1, ..., L\} \; and \; k \in \{1, ..., K\} \label{eq:cqn_as_formulation}\end{equation} We optimize our critic network with the following objective: \begin{equation}\sum\nolimits_{k}\sum\nolimits_{l}\left( Q_{\theta}^{l,k}(h_{t}, a_{t+k-1}^{l}, a_{t:t+K}^{l-1}) - r_{t+1} - \gamma \max_{a'}Q_{\bar{\theta}}^{l,k}(h_{t+1}, a', \pi^{l}_{K}(h_{t+1}) \right)^{2}, \label{eq:cqn_as_objective}\end{equation} where $\pi_{K ^{l}$ is an action sequence policy that outputs the action sequence $\mathbf{a _{t:t+K ^{l}$.}}}

elements.sent-2411-067: While the idea of using action sequence is simple, there are two important yet small details for effectively training RL agents with action sequence: (i) how we execute actions at each time step to control robots and (ii) how we store training data and sample batches for training.
elements.sent-2411-068: With the policy that outputs an action sequence $\mathbf{a _{t:t+K}$, one important question is how to execute actions at time step $i \in \{t, ..., t+K-1\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-069: For this, we use the idea of \citet{zhao2023learning that utilizes temporal ensemble, which computes $\mathbf{a _{t:t+K}$ every time step, saves it to a buffer, and executes a weighted average $\sum_{i w_{i \mathbf{a _{t-i / \sum w_{i}$ where $w_{i = \exp (-m * i)$ denotes a weight that assigns higher value to more recent actions.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-070: We find this scheme outperforms the alternative of computing $\mathbf{a _{t:t+K}$ every $K$ steps and executing each action for subsequent $K$ steps on most tasks we considered, except on several tasks that need reactive control.}
elements.paragraph-2411-007: Storing training data from environment interaction
elements.sent-2411-071: When storing samples from online environment interaction, we store a transition $(\mathbf{o _{t , \hat{\mathbf{a _{t , r_{t+1 , \mathbf{o _{t+1 )$ where $\hat{\mathbf{a _{t}$ denotes an action executed at time step $t$.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-072: For instance, if we use temporal ensemble for action execution, $\hat{\mathbf{a _{t}$ is a weighted average of action outputs obtained from previous $K$ time steps.}}
elements.paragraph-2411-008: Sampling training data from a replay buffer
elements.sent-2411-073: When sampling training data from the replay buffer, we sample a transition with action sequence, i.e., $(\mathbf{o _{t , \hat{\mathbf{a _{t:t+K , r_{t+1 , \mathbf{o _{t+1 )$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-074: If we sample time step $t$ near the end of episode so that we do not have enough data to construct a full action sequence, we fill the action sequence with null actions.
elements.sent-2411-075: In particular, in position control where we specify the position of joints or end effectors, we repeat the action from the last step so that the agent learns not to change the position.
elements.sent-2411-076: On the other hand, in torque control where we specify the force to apply to joints, we set the action after the last step to zero so that agent learns to not to apply force.

elements.sent-2411-078: These tasks with sparse and dense rewards, with or without vision sensors, and with or without demonstrations, allow for evaluating the capabilities and limitations of our algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-079: In particular, our experiments are designed to investigate the following questions:.
elements.sent-2411-080: Can CQN-AS quickly match the performance of a recent BC algorithm \citep{zhao2023learning and surpass it through online learning? How does CQN-AS compare to previous model-free RL algorithms \citep{haarnoja2018soft,yarats2022mastering,seo2024continuous ?.}}
elements.sent-2411-081: What is the contribution of each component in CQN-AS?.
elements.sent-2411-082: Under which conditions is CQN-AS effective? When does CQN-AS fail?.
elements.paragraph-2411-009: Baselines for fine-grained control tasks with demonstrations
elements.sent-2411-083: For tasks that need high-precision control, e.g., manipulation tasks from BiGym and RLBench, we consider model-free RL baselines that aim to learn deterministic policies, as we find that stochastic policies struggle to solve such fine-grained control tasks.
elements.sent-2411-084: Specifically, we consider (i) Coarse-to-fine Q-Network (CQN; \citealt{seo2024continuous ), a value-based RL algorithm that learns to zoom-into continuous action space in a coarse-to-fine manner, and (ii) DrQ-v2+, an optimized demo-driven variant of a model-free actor-critic algorithm DrQ-v2 \citep{yarats2022mastering that uses a deterministic policy algorithm and data augmentation.}}
elements.sent-2411-085: We further consider (iii) Action Chunking Transformer (ACT; \citealt{zhao2023learning ), a BC algorithm that trains a transformer \citep{vaswani2017attention policy to predict action sequence and utilizes temporal ensemble for executing actions, as our highly-optimized BC baseline.}}
elements.paragraph-2411-010: Baselines for whole-body control tasks with dense reward
elements.sent-2411-086: For locomotion tasks with dense reward, we consider (i) Soft Actor-Critic (SAC; \citealt{haarnoja2018soft ), a model-free actor-critic RL algorithm that maximizes action entropy, and (ii) Coarse-to-fine Q-Network (CQN; \citealt{seo2024continuous ).}}
elements.sent-2411-087: Moreover, although it is not the goal of this paper to compare against model-based RL algorithms, we also consider two model-based baselines: (iii) DreamerV3 \citep{hafner2023mastering , a model-based RL algorithm that learns a latent dynamics model and a policy using imagined trajectories and (iv) TD-MPC2 \citep{hansen2023td , a model-based RL algorithm that learns a latent dynamics model and utilizes local trajectory optimization in imagined latent trajectories.}}
elements.sent-2411-088: For training with expert demonstrations, we follow the setup of \citet{seo2024continuous .}
elements.sent-2411-089: Specifically, we keep a separate replay buffer that stores demonstrations and sample half of training data from demonstrations.
elements.sent-2411-090: We also relabel successful online episodes as demonstrations and store them in the demonstration replay buffer.
elements.sent-2411-091: For CQN-AS, we use an auxiliary BC loss from \citet{seo2024continuous based on large margin loss \citep{hester2018deep .}}
elements.sent-2411-092: For actor-critic baselines, we use an auxiliary BC loss that minimizes L2 loss between the policy outputs and expert actions.
elements.sent-2411-093: We implement CQN-AS based on a publicly available source code of CQN\footnote{\url{https://github.com/younggyoseo/CQN based on PyTorch \citep{paszke2019pytorch .}}}
elements.sent-2411-094: We will release the full source code upon publication.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=0.99\textwidth]{figures/results/bigym.pdf} \caption{BiGym results on 25 sparsely-rewarded mobile bi-manual manipulation tasks \citep{chernyadev2024bigym}. All experiments are initialized with 17 to 60 human-collected demonstrations, and RL methods are trained with an auxiliary BC objective. On many of the challenging long-horizon tasks, CQN-AS quickly learns to match the performance of ACT \citep{zhao2023learning} and surpass it through online learning, while other RL baselines fail to effectively accelerate training with noisy human-collected demonstrations. We report the success rate over 25 episodes. The solid line and shaded regions represent the mean and confidence intervals, respectively, across 4 runs. } \label{fig:bigym_experiments}
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=0.99\textwidth]{figures/results/humanoidbench.pdf} \caption{HumanoidBench results on 8 densely-rewarded humanoid control tasks \citep{sferrazza2024humanoidbench}. All the experiments start from scratch and RL methods do not have an auxiliary BC objective. CQN-AS significantly outperforms other model-free RL baselines on most tasks. CQN-AS often achieves competitive performance to model-based RL baselines, which is intriguing but not the main goal of this paper. For CQN-AS and CQN, we report the results aggregated over 4 runs. For other baselines, we report the results aggregated over 3 runs available from public website. The solid line and shaded regions represent the mean and confidence intervals.} \label{fig:humanoidbench_experiments}
elements.sent-2411-095: We study CQN-AS on mobile bi-manual manipulation tasks from BiGym \citep{chernyadev2024bigym .}
elements.sent-2411-096: BiGym's human-collected demonstrations are often noisy and multi-modal, posing challenges to RL algorithms which should effectively leverage demonstrations for solving sparsely-rewarded tasks.
elements.sent-2411-097: Because we find that not all demonstrations from BiGym benchmark can be successfully replayed\footnote{We use demonstrations available at the date of Oct 1st with the commit \tt{018f8b2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-098: We do not discard ones that fail to be replayed, but we use them as training data with zero reward because they can still be useful as failure experiences.
elements.sent-2411-099: To avoid training with too few demonstrations, we exclude the tasks where the ratio of successful demonstrations is below 50\%.
elements.sent-2411-100: This leaves us with 25 tasks, each with 17 to 60 demonstrations.
elements.sent-2411-101: For visual observations, we use RGB observations with 84$\times$84 resolution from head, left\_wrist, and right\_wrist cameras.
elements.sent-2411-102: We also use low-dimensional proprioceptive states from proprioception, proprioception\_grippers, and proprioception\_floating\_base sensors.
elements.sent-2411-103: We use (i) absolute joint position control action mode and (ii) floating base that replaces locomotion with classic controllers.
elements.sent-2411-104: We use the same set of hyperparameters for all the tasks, in particular, we use action sequence of length 16.
elements.sent-2411-105: More details on BiGym experiments are available in \cref{appendix:experimental_details .}
elements.sent-2411-106: We find that CQN-AS quickly matches the performance of ACT and outperforms it through online learning on 20/25 tasks, while other RL algorithms fail to do so especially on challenging long-horizon tasks such as Move Plate and Saucepan To Hob.
elements.sent-2411-107: A notable result here is that CQN-AS enables solving these challenging BiGym tasks while other RL baselines, i.e., CQN and DrQ-v2+, completely fail as they achieve 0\% success rate.
elements.sent-2411-108: This result highlights the capability of CQN-AS to accelerate RL training from noisy, multi-modal demonstrations collected by humans.
elements.sent-2411-109: However, we find that CQN-AS struggles to achieve meaningful success rate on some of the long-horizon tasks that require interaction with delicate objects such as cup or cutlery, leaving room for future work to incorporate advanced vision encoders \citep{he2016deep,he2022masked or critic architectures \citep{kapturowski2022human,chebotar2023q,springenberg2024offline .}}
elements.sent-2411-110: To show that CQN-AS can be generally applicable to tasks without demonstrations, we study CQN-AS on densely-rewarded humanoid control tasks from HumanoidBench \citep{sferrazza2024humanoidbench .}
elements.sent-2411-111: For HumanoidBench, we follow a standard setup that trains RL agents from scratch, which is also used in original benchmark \citep{sferrazza2024humanoidbench .}
elements.sent-2411-112: Specifically, we use low-dimensional states consisting of proprioception and privileged task information as inputs.

elements.sent-2411-143: In the context of reinforcement learning, \citet{medini2019mimicking proposed to pre-compute frequent action sequences from expert demonstrations and augment the action space with these sequences.}
elements.sent-2411-144: However, this idea introduces additional complexity and is not scalable to setups without demonstrations.

elements.sent-2411-148: In contrast, our work shows that training RL agents with action sequence is feasible and leads to improved performance compared to prior RL algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-149: We presented Coarse-to-fine Q-Network with Action Sequence (CQN-AS), a value-based RL algorithm that trains a critic network that outputs Q-values over action sequences.
elements.sent-2411-150: Extensive experiments in benchmarks with various setups show that our idea not only improves the performance of the base algorithm but also allows for solving complex tasks where prior RL algorithms completely fail.
elements.sent-2411-151: We believe our work will be strong evidence that shows RL can realize its promise to develop robots that can continually improve through online trial-and-error experiences, surpassing the performance of BC approaches.

elements.sent-2411-199: In reinforcement learning (RL), we train a value function to understand the long-term consequence of executing a single action.
elements.sent-2411-200: However, the value of taking each action can be ambiguous in robotics as robot movements are typically the aggregate result of executing multiple small actions.
elements.sent-2411-201: Moreover, robotic training data often consists of noisy trajectories, in which each action is noisy but executing a series of actions results in a meaningful robot movement.
elements.sent-2411-202: This further makes it difficult for the value function to understand the effect of individual actions.
elements.sent-2411-203: To address this, we introduce Coarse-to-fine Q-Network with Action Sequence (CQN-AS), a novel value-based RL algorithm that learns a critic network that outputs Q-values over a sequence of actions, i.e., explicitly training the value function to learn the consequence of executing action sequences.
elements.sent-2411-204: We study our algorithm on 53 robotic tasks with sparse and dense rewards, as well as with and without demonstrations, from BiGym, HumanoidBench, and RLBench.
elements.sent-2411-205: We find that CQN-AS outperforms various baselines, in particular on humanoid control tasks.
elements.sent-2411-207: Reinforcement learning (RL) holds the promise of continually improving policies through online experiences \citep{sutton2018reinforcement .}
elements.sent-2411-208: However, training RL agents on robotic tasks often requires a prohibitively large number of training samples \citep{kalashnikov2018scalable,herzog2023deep , which is problematic as deploying robots incurs a huge cost.}
elements.sent-2411-209: We posit that one cause for the poor data-efficiency of RL for robotics is the credit assignment problem \citep{minsky1961steps .}
elements.sent-2411-210: In value-based RL, we typically train a value function to understand the long-term consequences of taking individual actions.
elements.sent-2411-211: However, this becomes challenging in robotics as robot movements are the aggregate result of a sequence of low-level actions, where the effect of each action is often ambiguous.
elements.sent-2411-212: For instance, moving a robot arm forward involves multiple steps of changing joint angles.
elements.sent-2411-213: While one may easily understand the consequence of executing a series of actions, it is often difficult to learn how each low-level action, such as changing the joint angle by certain degrees, contributes to the resulting movement.
elements.figure-2411-012: \includegraphics[width=0.16\textwidth]{figures/high_level/rlbench_distribution.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.16\textwidth]{figures/high_level/bigym_distribution.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.15\textwidth]{figures/high_level/smoothing.pdf} \caption{ Noisy trajectories in robotic data. We visualize the $(x, y, z)$ coordinates of a gripper in demonstrations from RLBench \citep{james2020rlbench} that uses motion-planning for generating demonstrations and BiGym \citep{chernyadev2024bigym} that provides human-collected demonstrations. (Right) We report the success rates of replaying BiGym demonstrations with various action smoothing schemes. We find that na\"ive approach of smoothing actions can make demonstrations be invalid as smoothed actions often lose precision, highlighting the need for developing RL algorithms that can learn from noisy robotic training data. } \label{fig:noisy_trajectories}
elements.sent-2411-214: Moreover, robotic training data often consists of noisy trajectories, amplifying the credit assignment problem.
elements.sent-2411-215: For instance, we often initialize training with human-collected demonstrations that consist of noisy multi-modal trajectories \citep{chernyadev2024bigym .}
elements.sent-2411-216: In such trajectories, each action may be noisy because of human errors; however, executing a sequence of actions results in meaningful robot movement.
elements.sent-2411-217: One may think that this issue can be resolved by smoothing actions, but it often makes demonstrations be invalid by reducing action precision (see \cref{fig:noisy_trajectories ).}
elements.sent-2411-218: Moreover, when training RL agents, we typically inject some noise into actions for exploration \citep{sehnke2010parameter,lillicrap2016continuous , which induces trajectories with jerky motions.}
elements.sent-2411-219: This characteristic of robotic training data further makes it difficult to learn the value function that can properly evaluate the long-term consequence of each individual action.
elements.sent-2411-220: On the other hand, recent behavior cloning (BC; \citealt{pomerleau1988alvinn ) approaches have shown that predicting a sequence of actions enables policies to effectively approximate the noisy, multi-modal distribution of expert demonstrations \citep{zhao2023learning,chi2023diffusion .}}
elements.sent-2411-221: Inspired by this, we investigate whether we can similarly train a value function to explicitly learn the consequence of executing action sequences.
elements.sent-2411-222: As a straightforward implementation of this idea, in \cref{fig:actor_critic_value_overestimation , we train actor-critic algorithms \citep{haarnoja2018soft,fujimoto2018addressing with action sequence on stand task from HumanoidBench \citep{sferrazza2024humanoidbench .}}}
elements.sent-2411-223: Specifically, we train the actor to output action sequence and the critic to take action sequence as inputs instead of single-step actions.
elements.sent-2411-224: Unfortunately, we observe that actor-critic algorithms with action sequence suffer from a severe value overestimation.
elements.sent-2411-225: This is because a wider action space makes the critic more vulnerable to function approximation error \citep{fujimoto2018addressing and the actor can easily exploit the estimation error.}
elements.sent-2411-226: These results motivate us to design our RL algorithm with action sequence upon a recent value-based algorithm, i.e., Coarse-to-fine Q-Network (CQN; \citealt{seo2024continuous ), which solves continuous control tasks with discrete actions.}
elements.sent-2411-227: We find that training this critic-only algorithm with action sequence enables us to achieve the benefits of RL with action sequence yet avoid the value overestimation problem.

elements.figure-2411-014: \begin{subfigure}{0.5657\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/high_level/cqn_as_inference.pdf} \caption{Coarse-to-fine inference procedure} \label{fig:cqn_as_overview_inference} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.4243\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/high_level/cqn_as_architecture.pdf} \caption{Architecture} \label{fig:cqn_as_overview_architecture} \end{subfigure} \caption{Coarse-to-fine Q-Network with Action Sequence (CQN-AS). We build our algorithm upon Coarse-to-fine Q-Network (CQN; \citealt{seo2024continuous}), a recent critic-only RL algorithm that solves continuous control tasks with discrete actions. (a) In CQN framework, we train RL agents to zoom-into the continuous action space by iterating the procedures of (i) discretizing continuous action space into $B$ bins and (ii) find the bin with the highest Q-value to further discretize at the next level. (b) We train a critic network to output Q-values over a sequence of actions. We design our architecture to first obtain features for each sequence step and aggregate features from multiple sequence steps with a recurrent network. We then project these outputs into Q-values. } \label{fig:cqn_as_overview}
elements.sent-2411-234: Coarse-to-fine Q-Network (CQN; \citealt{seo2024continuous ) is a value-based RL algorithm that solves continuous control tasks with discrete actions.}
elements.sent-2411-235: The main idea of CQN is to train an RL agent to learn to select coarse discrete actions in shallower levels with larger bin sizes, and then refine their choices by selecting finer-grained actions in deeper levels with smaller bin sizes.
elements.sent-2411-236: Specifically, CQN iterates the procedures of (i) discretizing the continuous action space into multiple bins and (ii) selecting the bin with the highest Q-value to further discretize.
elements.sent-2411-237: This reformulates the continuous control problem as a multi-level discrete control problem, allowing for the use of ideas from sample-efficient discrete RL algorithms \citep{mnih2015human,silver2017mastering for continuous control.}

elements.sent-2411-250: We design our coarse-to-fine critic network to consist of multiple Q-networks that compute Q-values for each action at sequence step $k \in \{1, ..., K\}$ and level $l \in \{1, ..., L\}$: \begin{equation}Q_{\theta}^{l, k}(h_{t}, a_{t:t+K}^{l-1}) = \left[Q_{\theta}^{l, k}(h_{t}, a_{t+k-1}^{l,b}, a_{t:t+K}^{l-1})\right]_{b=1}^{B}\end{equation} where $B$ is the number of bins for each level.

elements.sent-2411-333: Predicting a sequence of actions has been crucial in the success of recent behavior cloning algorithms in robotics.
elements.sent-2411-334: Can similar ideas improve reinforcement learning (RL)? We answer affirmatively by observing that incorporating action sequences when predicting ground-truth return-to-go leads to lower validation loss.
elements.sent-2411-335: Motivated by this, we introduce Coarse-to-fine Q-Network with Action Sequence (CQN-AS), a novel value-based RL algorithm that learns a critic network that outputs Q-values over a sequence of actions, i.e., explicitly training the value function to learn the consequence of executing action sequences.
elements.sent-2411-336: Our experiments show that CQN-AS outperforms several baselines on a variety of sparse-reward humanoid control and tabletop manipulation tasks from BiGym and RLBench.
elements.figure-2411-023: \includegraphics[width=0.32\linewidth]{figures/tasks_teaser.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.64\linewidth]{figures/results/aggregate.pdf} \caption{Summary of results. Coarse-to-fine Q-Network with Action Sequence (CQN-AS) learns a critic network with action sequence. CQN-AS outperforms various RL and BC baselines such as CQN \citep{seo2024continuous}, DrQ-v2+ \citep{yarats2022mastering}, and ACT \citep{zhao2023learning} on 45 robotic tasks from BiGym \citep{chernyadev2024bigym} and RLBench \citep{james2020rlbench}.} \label{fig:summary_of_results}
elements.sent-2411-337: Predicting action sequences from expert trajectories is a key idea in recent successful behavior cloning (BC; \citealt{pomerleau1988alvinn ) approaches in robotics.}
elements.sent-2411-338: This has enabled policies to effectively approximate the noisy, multi-modal distribution of expert demonstrations \citep{zhao2023learning,chi2023diffusion .}
elements.sent-2411-339: Can this idea similarly be useful for reinforcement learning (RL)? Our initial finding is affirmative: we make an intriguing observation that using action sequences can enhance value learning.
elements.sent-2411-340: Specifically, with humanoid demonstrations from BiGym \citep{chernyadev2024bigym , we train regression models that predict the ground-truth return-to-go, i.e., the sum of discounted future rewards from the timestep $t$, given the current observation and action.}
elements.sent-2411-341: In \cref{fig:return_to_go_prediction , we find that using an action sequence $\mathbf{a _{t:t+K = \{\mathbf{a _{t , ..., \mathbf{a _{t+K-1}\}$ as input results in lower validation losses than using a single-step action $\mathbf{a _{t}$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-342: We hypothesize this is because action sequences, which can correspond to behavioral primitives such as going straight, make it easier for the model to learn the long-term outcomes compared to evaluating the effect of individual single-step actions.
elements.sent-2411-343: Building on this observation, we train actor-critic algorithms \citep{haarnoja2018soft,fujimoto2018addressing with action sequence on stand task from HumanoidBench \citep{sferrazza2024humanoidbench .}}
elements.sent-2411-344: However, we find these algorithms with action sequences suffer from severe value overestimation (see \cref{fig:value_overestimation ) and completely fail to solve the task (see \cref{fig:value_overestimation_performance ).}}
elements.sent-2411-345: This is because a wider action space makes the critic more vulnerable to function approximation error \citep{fujimoto2018addressing and the actor excessively maximizes value functions by exploiting this estimation error.}

elements.sent-2411-346: This result motivates us to design our RL algorithm with action sequence upon a recent critic-only algorithm, i.e., Coarse-to-fine Q-Network (CQN; \citealt{seo2024continuous ), which solves continuous control tasks with discrete actions.}
elements.sent-2411-347: Because there is no separate actor that may exploit value functions, i.e., CQN simply selects discrete actions with the highest Q-values, we find that training with action sequences is stable and thus avoids value overestimation problem.
elements.sent-2411-348: Our experiments show that CQN-AS improves the performance of CQN on sparse-reward humanoid control tasks from BiGym benchmark \citep{chernyadev2024bigym that provides human-collected demonstrations and sparse-reward tabletop manipulation tasks from RLBench \citep{james2020rlbench that provide demonstrations generated via motion-planning.}}
elements.sent-2411-349: Considering that CQN-AS is a critic-only algorithm that selects actions with the highest Q-value without a separate actor network, these results highlight the benefit of using action sequences in value learning.
elements.sent-2411-350: Our contributions can be summarized as below:.
elements.sent-2411-351: We make an observation that shows using action sequences can be useful for RL by enhancing value learning.
elements.sent-2411-352: We also show that standard actor-critic algorithms \citep{haarnoja2018soft,fujimoto2018addressing suffer from value overestimation when trained with action sequences.}
elements.sent-2411-353: We introduce Coarse-to-fine Q-Network with Action Sequence (CQN-AS) that trains a critic network to output Q-values over action sequences.
elements.sent-2411-354: This critic-only algorithm successfully avoids value overestimation problem and enhances the base CQN algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-355: In a demo-driven RL setup that initializes training with expert demonstrations, we show that CQN-AS surpasses the performance of ACT \citep{zhao2023learning -- a BC algorithm that trains a Transformer \citep{vaswani2017attention to predict action sequences.}}

CQN-AS extends Coarse-to-Fine Q-Network (CQN; \citealt{seo2024continuous}), a critic-only RL algorithm for continuous control using discretized actions. (a) CQN progressively zooms into the action space by discretizing it into $B$ bins and finding the bin with the highest Q-value to further discretize at the next level. (b) We train a critic to predict Q-values over entire action sequences by extracting per-step features and aggregating them with a recurrent network before projection to Q-values. } \label{fig:cqn_as_overview}
